goal: Maximize clinician usefulness while minimizing patient effort
principle: Ask only the questions needed to determine safety gates, medication fit, side-effect risk, and titration velocity.

clinician_output.key_decision: Start, hold, slow titration, micro-increase, switch medication pathway, or request clinician review
clinician_output.language: The software flags and suggests; the clinician decides.

titration_velocity_v.description: Higher score means faster standard titration may be reasonable. Lower score means slower titration, hold, or more monitoring.

interpretation.0.80_to_1.00: Standard titration likely acceptable if clinician agrees
interpretation.0.15_to_0.34: Hold longer at each step; clinician review before escalation
interpretation.0.00_to_0.14: Do not escalate; clinician review required

risk_scores.medication_fit: GLP_candidate | GLP_with_slow_titration | naltrexone_bupropion_review | phentermine_review | lifestyle_first | clinician_review_required

questions.patient_text: What is your age?

then.doctor_note: Older adult: consider slower dose pacing, closer hydration/GI monitoring, and muscle-protection counseling.

questions.patient_text: What sex were you assigned at birth?
options: Intersex / prefer to discuss with clinician

then.doctor_note: Female patient: ask reproductive safety and optional cycle-context questions.

questions.patient_text: What is your height and current weight?

then.doctor_note: BMI or eligibility criteria require clinician review before treatment.

questions.patient_text: Have you or anyone in your family had medullary thyroid cancer or MEN2?

then.patient_message: Thanks. Your clinician needs to review this before a GLP-1 plan can be created.
then.doctor_note: MTC/MEN2 history or uncertainty: clinician review required before prescribing.

questions.patient_text: Are you pregnant, breastfeeding, or trying to become pregnant in the next 2 months?

then.doctor_note: Pregnancy, lactation, or conception planning requires clinician review before GLP therapy.

questions.patient_text: What is your current reproductive status?

then.doctor_note: CRITICAL: Patient claims active menses at age >= 55. Rule out data entry error vs. postmenopausal bleeding. If on oral cyclical HRT, note that oral estrogens compound hepatic SHBG, increasing nausea risk.
Patient on cyclical HRT. Exogenous oral estrogens amplify GLP-1 nausea. Consider suggesting transition to transdermal estrogen delivery to bypass hepatic first-pass and reduce GI sensitivity.

questions.patient_text: If you have periods, when did your last period start?

then.doctor_note: Cycle timing available as optional context for nausea patterns and dose-escalation timing.
No cycle-based timing recommendation generated.

questions.patient_text: About how long is your usual cycle?

then.doctor_note: Cycle timing is too uncertain for escalation timing; use symptom check-ins instead.
If patient has recurring cycle-linked nausea, clinician may avoid escalation during predicted sensitive windows.

questions.patient_text: Do you currently use an oral birth control pill?

then.doctor_note: If tirzepatide is used, counsel on non-oral or backup contraception after initiation and dose escalation.

questions.patient_text: Have you ever had pancreatitis, gallbladder problems, or severe stomach-emptying problems?

if_then.if: answer contains 'Pancreatitis' || answer contains 'Gastroparesis / severe slow stomach emptying'

then.doctor_note: Major GI history: clinician review required before GLP start or escalation.

if_then.if: answer contains 'Gallstones / gallbladder attacks' || answer contains 'Gallbladder removed'

then.doctor_note: Gallbladder history: counsel on abdominal pain red flags and monitor during weight loss.

questions.patient_text: Do you take insulin or a sulfonylurea medication such as glipizide, glyburide, or glimepiride?

then.doctor_note: Diabetes medication interaction risk: review hypoglycemia risk and possible dose adjustments.

questions.patient_text: Do you take daily oral medications for Thyroid (e.g., Synthroid/levothyroxine), Depression/Anxiety (e.g., SSRIs, SNRIs), or Blood Pressure?

if_then.if: answer contains 'Thyroid medication' || answer contains 'Mental health medication (antidepressant/anti-anxiety)'

then.doctor_note: Pharmacokinetic Alert: GLP-1 delayed gastric emptying shifts the Tmax of co-administered oral drugs. Monitor for breakthrough symptoms (e.g., sudden fatigue, mood crashes, thyroid symptoms) during the first 6 weeks as steady-state absorption curves shift. Enforce taking thyroid meds 60 min before food and coffee.
Blood pressure medication noted. GLP-1 weight loss and GI effects may alter antihypertensive requirements over time. Monitor blood pressure at check-ins.

questions.patient_text: What medication format will you use?

then.doctor_note: Pen workflow: guide injection timing and technique; do not show syringe unit conversion.
Vial workflow: patient may need syringe fill-line visualization and dose-volume support.
Oral workflow: emphasize administration timing, absorption rules, and adherence.
Patient is unsure of delivery mode; clinician or care team should confirm before first dose.

questions.patient_text: How confident do you feel about giving yourself an injection?

then.doctor_note: Patient may benefit from walkthrough, video, or nurse-support message.
Schedule injection teaching before first dose.

questions.patient_text: Is this your first time using a prefilled injection pen?

then.doctor_note: Pen patient: provide medication-label confirmation and injection walkthrough. Avoid any partial-dose or click-count instructions.

questions.patient_text: How confident are you drawing medicine into a syringe?

then.doctor_note: Vial patient: show exact syringe fill-line and consider teaching call.

questions.patient_text: After a normal meal, do you often feel physically hungry again within 1–2 hours?

then.doctor_note: Early hunger return may indicate strong appetite-response potential but also a need to avoid abrupt GI/satiety change.
Track food-noise and hunger response after first doses.

questions.patient_text: Before you feel full, can you eat a much larger meal than most people?

then.doctor_note: Large meal capacity: counsel on meal-size reduction before escalation; monitor whether satiety improves.

questions.patient_text: Do cravings or food noise make it hard to stop thinking about food?

then.doctor_note: Food-noise baseline established. If food noise resolves at low dose, clinician may hold at minimal effective dose instead of escalating.

questions.patient_text: Do stress, boredom, or emotions often trigger eating even when you are not physically hungry?

then.doctor_note: Emotional eating pattern: consider behavioral support and clinician review of non-GLP or adjunctive reward-pathway options.

questions.patient_text: When during the week do you find it hardest to manage cravings, 'food noise', or emotional eating?
options: Evenings every day / No specific day pattern

then.doctor_note: Chronobiological Match: Recommend a Wednesday evening injection day. This aligns peak plasma concentration (Cmax) at 24-48 hours post-dose directly with their weekend behavioral vulnerability window.
Chronobiological Match: Recommend a Saturday or Sunday injection day to align Cmax window with mid-week vulnerability.

if_then.if: answer == 'Evenings every day / No specific day pattern'

questions.patient_text: Before medication, how often do you have heartburn, reflux, or burping after meals?

then.doctor_note: Moderate reflux baseline: include reflux prevention guidance and avoid escalation if symptoms worsen.
High reflux risk: clinician should consider slower titration and active reflux management before escalation.

questions.patient_text: How often do you have constipation or fewer than 3 bowel movements per week?

then.doctor_note: Constipation baseline is high. Add prevention guidance and avoid escalation if constipation is active.

questions.patient_text: How many hours typically pass between your last bite of food and the time you lie down for bed?

if_then.if: answer == 'Less than 2 hours' || answer == '2 to 4 hours'

then.doctor_note: Circadian Motility Conflict: Natural nightly reduction in vagal tone combined with GLP-1 gastric stasis will trap late meals in the stomach, causing nighttime reflux or morning vomiting. Before increasing dose, enforce a strict 4-hour pre-sleep fasting window.

questions.patient_text: Are you prone to nausea, motion sickness, strong-smell sensitivity, or vomiting from anesthesia or medications?

then.doctor_note: Moderate nausea sensitivity: use 72-hour check-in and avoid escalation if nausea persists.
High nausea sensitivity: consider slower dose pacing, proactive nausea counseling, and clinician review before escalation.

questions.patient_text: How would you describe your baseline sense of smell and taste compared to other people?

then.doctor_note: Sensory Cortex Flag: High gustatory/olfactory sensitivity makes patient highly prone to medication-induced dysgeusia (metallic water taste, intense food aversions). If target protein intake drops, pivot immediately to flavor-neutral whey/pea isolates and cold-filtered water.

questions.patient_text: Do you get dizzy, lightheaded, or feel your heart race when standing up quickly?

then.doctor_note: Possible hydration/autonomic risk: track dizziness, fluid intake, vomiting/diarrhea, and heart-rate symptoms.
High dizziness/autonomic symptom burden: clinician review before fast escalation; avoid stimulant-pathway suggestions unless clinician determines appropriate.

questions.patient_text: Do you regularly wear a smart device (e.g., Apple Watch, Oura Ring, Garmin, Fitbit) that tracks resting heart rate or overnight Heart Rate Variability (HRV)?

then.doctor_note: Digital Autonomic Guardrail: Patient tracks biometrics. Advise monitoring overnight HRV; a sustained baseline drop of >25% post-injection acts as a leading indicator of autonomic stress before physical GI symptoms manifest.

questions.patient_text: On a typical day, how much fluid do you drink?

then.doctor_note: Low hydration readiness: fatigue/dizziness may worsen during appetite suppression or GI side effects.

questions.patient_text: How active are you most weeks?

then.doctor_note: Activity or age suggests need to screen for functional strength and muscle-protection readiness.

questions.patient_text: Can you stand up from a chair without using your arms?

then.doctor_note: Moderate functional risk: add protein and resistance-training support.
High functional risk: clinician should monitor strength, falls, fatigue, and excessive appetite suppression.

questions.patient_text: Independent of your weight, how would you describe your typical baseline physical energy levels over the last month?
options: Prone to heavy fatigue or sluggishness
Normal / Steady energy throughout the day

if_then.if: answer == 'Prone to heavy fatigue or sluggishness' && q_hungry_soon_after_meals.answer == 'Often'

then.doctor_note: Mitochondrial Lock Risk: High 'Hungry Gut' signal matching baseline fatigue. Rapid GLP-1-induced lipolysis can overwhelm cellular beta-oxidation pathways. Proactively suggest Mitochondrial Co-Factors (CoQ10 200mg + L-Carnitine 1,000mg) to decouple drug-induced fatigue from therapeutic benefit.

if_then.if: answer == 'Prone to heavy fatigue or sluggishness'

then.doctor_note: Elevated fatigue baseline: monitor energy levels closely during titration; distinguish drug-induced fatigue from caloric restriction.

questions.patient_text: Have you ever had a gout flare, kidney stones, or sudden intense pain or swelling in a single joint (such as your big toe or knee)?

if_then.if: answer == 'Yes, diagnosed gout' || answer == 'Yes, unexplained sudden joint swelling'

then.doctor_note: Kinetic Excretion Risk: Rapid GLP-1-driven lipolysis releases ketones and free fatty acids that directly compete with uric acid filtration at the renal tubule. High titration velocity may precipitate an acute gout flare. Mandate a strict 3L daily hydration floor and consider baseline uric acid lab before first escalation.
Kidney stone history: enforce hydration protocol and monitor for flank pain during rapid weight loss phases.

questions.patient_text: How many days per week do you do strength or resistance exercise?

then.doctor_note: No resistance training: include beginner strength plan to support quality of weight loss.

questions.patient_text: Do you usually get protein at most meals?

then.doctor_note: Low protein readiness: clinician may set individualized protein goal or dietitian referral.

questions.patient_text: Over the last 2 weeks, how often have you had little interest or pleasure in doing things?

if_then.if: answer == 'More than half the days' || answer == 'Nearly every day'

then.doctor_note: Baseline Hedonic Tone Vulnerability: Central dopamine downregulation risk. If weight declines but generalized life pleasure plummets, enforce an absolute Dose Lock at the Minimal Effective Dose — do not prioritize scale weight over severe anhedonia.

questions.patient_text: Over the last 2 weeks, how often have you felt down, depressed, or hopeless?

if_then.if: answer == 'More than half the days' || answer == 'Nearly every day'

then.doctor_note: Depression screen positive: clinician review and mood monitoring recommended.

questions.patient_text: Have you taken a GLP-1 medication before?
options: Yes, stopped due to side effects
Yes, stopped because it did not work well

then.doctor_note: Prior tolerance suggests lower initial uncertainty; still monitor symptoms after escalation.

if_then.if: answer == 'Yes, stopped due to side effects'

then.doctor_note: Prior side-effect discontinuation: use slow re-initiation, close check-ins, and clinician-reviewed hold/reduce plan.

if_then.if: answer == 'Yes, stopped because it did not work well'

then.doctor_note: Prior low response: clarify dose reached, adherence, duration, food-noise response, and medication choice before assuming non-response.
Access barrier: consider affordability, medication format, and continuity plan.

questions.patient_text: What was the hardest side effect last time?

then.doctor_note: Prior GI intolerance: create pre-dose prevention plan and avoid automatic escalation.
Prior mood change: clinician should monitor mood and consider alternative pathway if risk is high.

questions.patient_text: What was the highest dose you tolerated, if you remember?

then.doctor_note: Use prior tolerated dose as context; verify medication, dose, and duration.

questions.patient_text: How important is keeping medication cost low?

then.doctor_note: Budget-sensitive patient: clinician may review lower-cost oral options if clinically appropriate.

questions.patient_text: Would you strongly prefer an oral medication over an injection?

then.doctor_note: Oral preference plus emotional eating: consider naltrexone/bupropion review if clinically appropriate.
Oral preference and budget sensitivity: clinician may review lower-cost oral options, with cardiovascular and psychiatric screening.

questions.patient_text: How important are fast short-term results?

then.doctor_note: Expectation-setting needed. Clinician may discuss short-term vs long-term medication options and safety tradeoffs.

then.doctor_output: Contraindication or major safety concern requires review before plan creation.
Show cycle context as optional escalation timing context, not a hard rule.
then.patient_effort_saved: Do not ask daily cycle questions unless symptoms recur.

then.patient_app: Show pen confirmation and injection walkthrough.
then.doctor_output: Dose pacing options are timing-based: repeat, hold, delay, or escalate after clinician approval.

then.patient_app: Show vial label confirmation and exact syringe fill-line visual.
then.doctor_output: Dose pacing can include smaller unit-based changes if the clinician approves.

then.doctor_recommendation: Slow titration, 72-hour check-in, no escalation with active symptoms.
Hold current dose or delay escalation; clinician review required.
then.patient_message: Your clinician will review your symptoms before any dose increase.

then.doctor_recommendation: Continue current plan or consider cautious micro-increase if clinician agrees.
Hydration/electrolyte plan and closer monitoring before escalation.
Consider behavioral support or naltrexone/bupropion review if clinically appropriate.
then.doctor_note: Do not assume GLP alone will address emotional triggers.

then.doctor_recommendation: Clinician-only review for short-term oral appetite-suppression option.
then.patient_facing_language: Your clinician may discuss lower-cost oral options if appropriate.

then.doctor_recommendation: Consider clinician-selected nausea/motility management pathway based on local regulations and patient risk.
then.doctor_note: Domperidone should not be presented as an automatic protocol. If considered, it requires jurisdiction-specific clinician judgment and safety review.

then.doctor_recommendation: Inject Wednesday evenings to bracket peak vulnerability window with maximum drug concentration (Cmax at 24-48h aligns with Friday–Sunday).
then.patient_instruction: Your clinician may recommend a Wednesday evening injection time to help you feel your best over the weekend.
then.doctor_note: Chronobiological optimization: aligning Cmax with behavioral vulnerability window reduces breakthrough cravings at the peak risk period.

then.doctor_recommendation: Initiate HRV/RHR wearable compliance loop. Request patient share overnight HRV trend data at each check-in.
then.doctor_note: Digital Autonomic Guardrail active: a sustained HRV baseline drop of >25% is a leading indicator of autonomic stress — flag for clinician review before next dose escalation.

then.doctor_recommendation: Enforce Minimal Effective Dose lock. Block automatic dose escalation if patient reports flat mood, general anhedonia, or loss of life pleasure — even if weight loss is progressing.
then.doctor_note: Hedonic Tone Protection Rule: do not sacrifice dopaminergic reward baseline for scale weight. If anhedonia surfaces or worsens at any dose, lock at MEDose and initiate clinician review.

alternatives: phentermine review only if budget/oral/short-term priority and no stimulant risk

example.action: Approve starter plan with slow dose pacing
rules: repeat same pen dose if symptoms persist
for vial patients, allow smaller clinician-approved micro-increases

target_injection_window.label: Recommended Injection Timing
target_injection_window.rationale: Aligns Cmax (24-48h post-dose) with identified weekend behavioral vulnerability window

mitochondrial_kinetic_status.label: Mitochondrial Risk
mitochondrial_kinetic_status.cofactor_indication: CoQ10 200mg + L-Carnitine 1,000mg recommended before dose escalation

digital_autonomic_buffer.label: Wearable Tracking
digital_autonomic_buffer.protocol: Monitor overnight HRV; flag if baseline drops >25% post-injection

pharmacokinetic_interferences.critical_tmax_shift: High — Thyroid/SSRI Tmax drift risk active
pharmacokinetic_interferences.hrt_routing: Cyclical HRT user — consider transdermal transition

patient_plan_generated: Bowel regularity plan if constipation risk
Muscle-preservation plan if chair-stand or sedentary risk

do_not_ask_everyone: chair stand unless older, sedentary, or strength risk
prior dose details unless prior GLP use
budget/oral preference unless access, injection anxiety, or medication-fit ambiguity
wearable biomarkers only if hydration/autonomic risk is moderate or high
sensory sensitivity only if nausea sensitivity is moderate or high
gout/joint history only if activity risk or rapid weight loss likely
patient_effort_reduction.clinician_value: Produces a treatment-planning report rather than raw form answers.
